preFix.title: Fitting a door lining to a new door opening
preFix.subtitle: Plumb and fix a softwood door lining in a new or re-opened partition wall opening
preFix.excerpt: How to fit a three-piece softwood door lining in a new partition opening, covering cutting the legs to length, plumbing and fixing to the studs, fitting the head, and adding the planted door stop after the door is hung.
preFix.sourceNotes: Newey and Drage Practical Carpentry and Joinery. Collins Complete DIY Manual. Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics.

content.text: A
door lining

must be plumb in both planes and square at the head corner. A lining that is out of plumb by more than 2 mm per metre means the hung door will swing open or closed under its own weight. The lining is fixed to the structural studs or head plate of the partition, not to the plasterboard skin.
What you will need: materials

Standard door lining stock: 32 mm or 38 mm PAR softwood, width to match the wall thickness. For a 100 mm stud partition with two layers of 12.5 mm plasterboard on each face, the lining width is 100 mm. Lining sets are sold pre-made. Planted door stop: 12 x 38 mm PAR softwood strip, length to run around three sides of the opening. No. 8 x 63 mm screws for fixing into the structural studs. PVA for any jointing.
Step 1, check and prepare the opening

Check the opening for plumb on both sides using the spirit level. The structural studs at each side of the opening must be plumb: if not, pack behind the lining leg with thin timber packers. Check the head plate for level. The opening must be tall enough to accept the door height plus the lining head thickness (32 or 38 mm) plus a 3 mm gap at the top of the door.
Step 2, cut the legs to length

Measure the finished floor-to-head height and cut both lining legs to this length. The legs sit on the finished floor surface: if the floor is not yet laid, add the floor thickness to the leg length and trim after the floor goes in. Cut the bottom of each leg square with the crosscut saw; check with the combination square. Both legs must be exactly the same length or the head will not be level.
Step 3, fix the first leg

Hold the first leg in position in the opening. Check it is plumb on its face and on its edge with the spirit level. Pack behind it with timber offcuts where needed to bring it plumb. Pre-drill and countersink, then drive No. 8 x 63 mm screws through the lining into the structural stud at 400 mm centres. The screws will be covered by the architrave: they can be through the face of the lining. Check plumb again after tightening each screw.
Step 4, fit the head and second leg

Cut the head piece to fit between the two legs. A housing joint cut into the leg top accepts the head end for a stronger joint; a simple butt joint glued and screwed from above is sufficient for most partition doors. Glue the head joints, check the head is level, and fix. Fit the second leg in the same way as the first, checking plumb and the internal width at the top, middle, and bottom: both checks must match to within 2 mm.
Step 5, fit the planted door stop
Hang the door first, then fit the
planted door stop

with the door closed. Hold the stop strip against the door face and nail it through the lining face at 300 mm centres with 38 mm oval nails. The stop must be flush with the door face with the door in the closed and latched position. Check the stop is tight against the door on both legs and across the head.
Variations

For a double-leaf door opening, fit a central mullion between the two door sets instead of a central stud. The mullion carries a planted stop on each face. For a masonry opening (brick or block wall rather than stud partition), fix the lining using plastic wall plugs and long screws into the masonry reveals, packing behind each leg to bring it plumb.
Care

If a door begins to catch on the stop after fitting, check whether the stop has moved or the lining has twisted. A single nail-head driven a little further will pull a stop in tight. Repaint the lining and stop at each room redecoration; bare softwood soaks up moisture from a newly plastered wall and may twist if left uncoated.

attrs.fix: Remove the lining leg, add or remove packing behind it to bring it plumb in both planes, and re-fix. This must be corrected before the architrave goes on; the architrave will hide the packing.
attrs.cause: The lining legs are not plumb; the hinge side is out of plumb toward or away from the door face.
attrs.symptom: Door swings open or closed after hanging, even on a level floor

attrs.fix: Check that both legs sit flat on the floor or subfloor. If one leg bottom is not flat, plane or sand it until the head is level. Recheck the leg plumb after adjustment.
attrs.cause: The two legs were cut to slightly different lengths, or one leg is sitting higher than the other on the floor.
attrs.symptom: Lining head is not level after both legs are fixed

attrs.fix: Check the internal width at the top, middle, and bottom. If the legs are bowed, repack behind the bowed leg at mid-height. The width must be consistent to within 2 mm for the door to hang correctly.
attrs.cause: The floor has a slight bow or the legs were set parallel at the top but not checked at mid-height.
attrs.symptom: Internal width of the lining is correct at the head but narrower at the bottom
